summary: Introduces three more cognate conversion rules: -ant/-ent to -ante/-ente, -ible/-able endings, and the -mente adverb suffix. Also teaches the rule that a /j/ sound in English cognates becomes /kh/ in Spanish.

teachings.summary: English words ending in -ant or -ent can be converted to Spanish by adding an -e on the end, making them -ante or -ente.
Adding -mente to an adjective creates an adverb, equivalent to -ly in English.
teachings.notes: 'Normalmente' can be used like 'usually'. 'Naturalmente' can be used like 'obviously'.

teachings.summary: When a convertible English word has a /j/ sound (not just the letter g), it becomes a /kh/ (fricative) sound in Spanish.
teachings.rule: The /j/ sound in English cognates becomes /kh/ (throat friction) in Spanish. A hard /g/ stays as /g/.
teachings.notes: Argentina is a good hook to remember this rule — the g in Argentina has the /kh/ sound.

teachings.summary: English words ending -ible or -able can be converted to Spanish by pronouncing them syllable by syllable with the accent second from the end.
teachings.rule: -ible/-able words convert to Spanish with same spelling, pronounced syllable by syllable with penultimate stress.
teachings.notes: This works when -able/-ible is truly part of the word (not coined words like 'doable').

teachings.summary: Continued practice using 'es' in sentences with new adjectives and adverbs.
